author: Alkarim Billawala / alkarim.billawala.ca
weeks: Week 30 · GI II — Diarrhea & Lower GI

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman has 2 days of watery diarrhea after a buffet — 6 loose stools/day, mild cramps, no fever, no blood, no recent antibiotics. She is thirsty but not dizzy with normal vitals, and wants to reduce diarrhea before an exam. Best approach?
options: Loperamide only if stool becomes bloody
Ciprofloxacin for all acute watery diarrhea
questions.explain: Acute non-bloody diarrhea without red flags is treated supportively with oral rehydration; loperamide is reasonable because invasive/bloody disease is not suspected.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man has acute bloody diarrhea, fever, and severe cramps after undercooked chicken — 10 stools/day, feeling lightheaded. A friend offers loperamide. Best advice?
options: Take loperamide; it shortens invasive diarrhea
Avoid anti-motility agents; focus on hydration and appropriate testing
questions.explain: Bloody diarrhea with fever suggests invasive/inflammatory colitis → avoid anti-motility agents (risk of toxic megacolon / HUS), hydrate, and get stool testing with targeted therapy.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman with autoimmune hypothyroidism has fatigue, bloating, and chronic loose non-bloody stools. Hb 102, MCV 72, ferritin 2. No overt bleeding; not vegetarian. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: Chronic non-bloody diarrhea + iron-deficiency anemia + an autoimmune background points to celiac disease, where duodenal injury impairs iron absorption. Confirm with tTG-IgA + total IgA while still eating gluten.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old with Crohn disease has had 180 cm of distal ileum resected. Her disease is controlled on a biologic, but she now has greasy, floating, foul stools and weight loss. What is the mechanism?
questions.explain: A large terminal-ileal resection depletes the bile-acid pool faster than the liver can replace it → impaired micelle formation → fat malabsorption (steatorrhea). The terminal ileum also absorbs B12.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old man has 3 months of diarrhea, RLQ cramping, 10-lb weight loss, nocturnal stools, aphthous ulcers, anemia, high CRP, low albumin. Stool culture negative; colonoscopy shows patchy terminal-ileal and right-colon inflammation; a perianal skin tag is noted. Diagnosis?
questions.explain: Crohn disease: RLQ pain, weight loss, nocturnal diarrhea, anemia, high CRP, low albumin, aphthous ulcers, patchy terminal-ileal/right-sided disease, and perianal findings (skip lesions, transmural).

questions.stem: A patient with moderate-severe Crohn disease started on prednisone improves dramatically and asks to stay on it long-term to prevent relapse. Best response?
options: Yes, prednisone is ideal for maintenance
No — prednisone is for induction, not maintenance, due to serious long-term toxicity
Prednisone is only used in UC
questions.explain: Corticosteroids induce remission but are not for maintenance — long-term toxicity (osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, hyperglycemia, infection, avascular necrosis). Transition to a steroid-sparing maintenance agent.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old nursing-home resident with dementia and chronic constipation (on iron) develops 'diarrhea' and fecal incontinence, with poor appetite and occasional vomiting. Most likely cause?
questions.explain: An older adult with a constipation history and new 'diarrhea'/incontinence has fecal impaction with overflow until proven otherwise (iron worsens constipation). Examine the rectum and disimpact — don't give anti-diarrheals.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman has years of recurrent abdominal pain associated with changes in stool frequency and form, sometimes relieved by defecation. No bleeding, anemia, nocturnal symptoms, weight loss, or family history of IBD/CRC. CBC and CRP normal. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: Chronic pain associated with defecation and stool changes, no alarm features, and normal inflammatory testing fits IBS (Rome IV). Limited testing (CBC, CRP/calprotectin, celiac serology) is reasonable.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old develops bloody diarrhea after a petting-zoo visit. Stool suggests Shiga-toxin E. coli (O157). Which management principle is most important?
options: Give loperamide to reduce stool frequency
Avoid antibiotics and anti-motility agents; hydrate and monitor for hemolytic-uremic syndrome
questions.explain: In suspected STEC/O157, both antibiotics and anti-motility agents may increase the risk of hemolytic-uremic syndrome. Manage supportively with hydration and monitor renal function, hemoglobin, and platelets.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old with chronic non-bloody diarrhea has a normal CBC and CRP. Which single stool test best separates likely inflammatory bowel disease from a functional disorder?
questions.explain: Fecal calprotectin reflects neutrophilic mucosal inflammation and is the key gatekeeper separating inflammatory (IBD) from functional (IBS) disease, guiding the need for colonoscopy.

questions.stem: A patient suspected of celiac disease has already started a gluten-free diet on her own and feels better. What is the most appropriate advice regarding testing?
options: Test serology now; the diet won't affect it
Resume a gluten-containing diet (gluten challenge) before serology and biopsy
Order HLA typing to confirm the diagnosis
questions.explain: Serology (tTG-IgA) and histology normalize on a gluten-free diet, so she must undergo a gluten challenge before testing for an accurate diagnosis. (HLA-DQ2/DQ8 can only exclude, not confirm.)

questions.stem: A patient with severe ulcerative colitis develops abdominal distension, fever, tachycardia, and a dilated colon on x-ray. Which complication is most concerning?
questions.explain: Severe UC can progress to toxic megacolon (colonic dilation + systemic toxicity), a surgical emergency. Avoid anti-motility/opioid agents, which can precipitate it. Colectomy is curative for refractory/complicated colitis.

questions.stem: Before starting infliximab for Crohn disease, which screening is essential?
options: Screen for latent tuberculosis and hepatitis B
questions.explain: Anti-TNF biologics reactivate latent infections, so screen for tuberculosis and hepatitis B (and update vaccinations) before starting. Live vaccines are contraindicated during immunosuppression.

questions.stem: A woman with IBS-C has not responded to fiber and PEG. Which agent specifically increases intestinal fluid secretion for constipation?
questions.explain: Linaclotide (a guanylate cyclase-C agonist secretagogue) increases intestinal fluid secretion and transit — used in IBS-C and chronic constipation. Loperamide would worsen constipation.

questions.stem: A patient with a prior limited ileal resection has chronic watery diarrhea that persists with fasting and improves with a bile-acid binder trial. What is the mechanism and treatment?
questions.explain: A smaller ileal resection spills bile acids into the colon, stimulating secretion → secretory bile-acid diarrhea (persists with fasting). A bile-acid sequestrant (cholestyramine) is both diagnostic and therapeutic.

questions.stem: A patient on chronic opioids for cancer pain has refractory constipation despite osmotic laxatives. Which agent specifically targets the mechanism?
questions.explain: Naloxegol (and methylnaltrexone) are peripherally-acting mu-opioid antagonists (PAMORAs) that block gut opioid receptors without reversing central analgesia — targeted treatment for opioid-induced constipation.

questions.stem: Which feature is characteristic of ulcerative colitis rather than Crohn disease?
options: Continuous colonic inflammation starting at the rectum
questions.explain: UC is continuous, mucosal, and begins in the rectum extending proximally within the colon. Skip lesions, transmural inflammation, perianal disease, and terminal-ileal involvement are Crohn features.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old develops new constipation. Which combination of findings should prompt a search for a secondary cause rather than starting laxatives alone?
options: Hypothyroidism, hypercalcemia, or new opioid use
questions.explain: Constipation can be secondary to hypothyroidism, hypercalcemia, diabetes, neurologic disease, and drugs (opioids, iron, anticholinergics, CCBs). New constipation with these clues (or alarm features) warrants a workup.

questions.stem: A patient's large-volume watery diarrhea continues unchanged during a 48-hour fast. Which mechanism does this suggest?
questions.explain: Secretory diarrhea persists despite fasting (driven by active secretion — enterotoxins, bile acids, neuroendocrine tumours). Osmotic diarrhea improves with fasting because the offending solute is removed.

questions.stem: Mesalamine (5-ASA) is most useful in which setting?
questions.explain: 5-ASA (mesalamine) is mainly effective in mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis (induction and maintenance); it has little role in Crohn disease.

cards.prompt: What two features classify diarrhea, and what mimics must be excluded?
cards.answer: Duration (acute <4 weeks vs chronic >4 weeks) and visible blood. Exclude mimics: overflow diarrhea from constipation, fecal incontinence, and hyperdefecation (thyrotoxicosis).

cards.prompt: How do osmotic and secretory diarrhea differ on fasting?
cards.answer: Osmotic improves with fasting (offending solute removed); secretory persists despite fasting (active secretion, large-volume watery stool).

cards.prompt: How is acute non-bloody diarrhea managed?
cards.answer: Oral rehydration first-line (IV if severe), diet as tolerated, and loperamide/diphenoxylate is reasonable since invasive disease isn't suspected. Investigate only if severe, persistent, immunocompromised, or outbreak/antibiotic-related.

cards.prompt: Why avoid anti-motility agents in bloody diarrhea?
cards.answer: Bloody diarrhea implies invasive/inflammatory colitis; slowing motility can worsen toxic megacolon and, in Shiga-toxin E. coli, increase hemolytic-uremic syndrome risk.

cards.prompt: What does the duodenum vs terminal ileum absorb, and the disease link?
cards.answer: Duodenum: iron and calcium (celiac → iron-deficiency anemia, bone disease). Terminal ileum: B12 and bile acids (Crohn/resection → B12 deficiency, bile-acid diarrhea or steatorrhea).

cards.prompt: What does fecal calprotectin distinguish?
cards.answer: Inflammatory disease (IBD) from functional disease (IBS) — it reflects neutrophilic mucosal inflammation and guides the need for colonoscopy.

cards.prompt: Why does celiac disease cause iron-deficiency anemia?
cards.answer: Gluten-triggered injury concentrates in the duodenum, where iron (and calcium) are preferentially absorbed, so iron deficiency anemia and bone disease are common presentations.

cards.prompt: What is the celiac testing pitfall?
cards.answer: Serology (tTG-IgA + total IgA) and biopsy normalize on a gluten-free diet — test while the patient is still eating gluten, or do a gluten challenge first.

cards.prompt: State the simplified Rome IV criteria for IBS.
cards.answer: Recurrent abdominal pain associated with ≥2 of: relation to defecation, change in stool frequency, change in stool form — over ≥6 months, with no alarm features.

cards.prompt: Which alarm features argue against IBS?
cards.answer: Rectal bleeding, anemia, weight loss, nocturnal symptoms, elevated inflammatory markers, family history of IBD/colorectal cancer, or persistent symptoms despite therapy.

cards.prompt: Contrast Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis anatomically.
cards.answer: Crohn: mouth-to-anus, skip lesions, transmural, often terminal ileum/right colon, perianal disease, fistulas. UC: colon only, continuous from the rectum, mucosal, bloody diarrhea, toxic-megacolon/cancer risk.

cards.prompt: Name extraintestinal manifestations of IBD.
cards.answer: Aphthous ulcers, episcleritis/uveitis, erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum, peripheral/axial arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, and primary sclerosing cholangitis.

cards.prompt: What is the role of corticosteroids in IBD?
cards.answer: Induction of remission only — not maintenance — because of serious long-term toxicity (osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, hyperglycemia, infection, avascular necrosis). Plan steroid-sparing maintenance.

cards.prompt: What must be screened before an anti-TNF biologic?
cards.answer: Latent tuberculosis and hepatitis B (reactivation risk); update vaccinations; avoid live vaccines during immunosuppression.

cards.prompt: What does new 'diarrhea' and incontinence in a constipated older adult suggest?
cards.answer: Fecal impaction with overflow diarrhea until proven otherwise — examine the rectum, disimpact, and avoid anti-diarrheals (iron worsens it).

cards.prompt: List secondary causes of constipation to exclude.
cards.answer: Opioids, iron, anticholinergics, calcium-channel blockers, hypothyroidism, hypercalcemia, diabetes, Parkinson disease, spinal-cord disease, obstruction, and defecatory disorders.

cards.prompt: Diarrhea that stops with fasting is most likely:
Loperamide should be avoided in:
The first-line serologic test for celiac disease is:
Disease of the terminal ileum classically causes deficiency of:
Which agent increases intestinal secretion for IBS-C?
Mesalamine is most effective in:
A feared complication of severe ulcerative colitis is:
In suspected Shiga-toxin E. coli, you should avoid:
Opioid-induced constipation is specifically treated with:
Select causes of acute bloody diarrhea:
Select features typical of Crohn disease:
Select biologic/advanced therapies used in IBD:
Select appropriate baseline tests for chronic diarrhea:
Select mimics to exclude before diagnosing true diarrhea:

cards.text: Diarrhea is acute if < {{4}} weeks and chronic if > {{4}} weeks.
cards.explain: Combined with bloody/non-bloody to form four categories.

cards.text: Celiac first-line serology is {{tissue transglutaminase IgA}} with {{total IgA}}, tested while eating {{gluten}}.
cards.explain: Total IgA catches selective IgA deficiency (false-negative tTG).

cards.text: Ulcerative colitis is {{continuous}} and {{colon}}-only from the rectum; Crohn has {{skip}} lesions and is transmural.
cards.explain: Depth and distribution drive complications and surgery.

cards.text: Corticosteroids are used for {{induction}} of remission in IBD, not {{maintenance}}.
cards.explain: Long-term steroid toxicity drives steroid-sparing maintenance.

cards.text: Oral rehydration works through {{sodium-glucose}} cotransport in the {{jejunum}}.
cards.explain: Glucose drives sodium and water absorption even in secretory states.

cards.prompt: Order the approach to diarrhea:
items: Classify: acute vs chronic, bloody vs non-bloody
Targeted testing and treatment by category

cards.prompt: Order the celiac diagnostic pathway:
items: Check tTG-IgA + total IgA while eating gluten

cards.prompt: Order the IBD treatment logic:
items: Screen TB + hepatitis B before immunosuppression
Surgery for complications (curative colectomy in UC)

cards.prompt: Match the diarrhea mechanism to an example:
Match the IBD drug to its role:
Match the bowel region to its deficiency when diseased:
Match the laxative to its class:
What is microscopic colitis and how is it diagnosed?
cards.answer: Chronic watery non-bloody diarrhea (often older women, sometimes drug-associated) with a normal-appearing colonoscopy but inflammation on biopsy — so biopsies are needed even when the mucosa looks normal.

cards.prompt: How is moderate-severe traveler's diarrhea managed?
cards.answer: Hydration plus a short antibiotic course (azithromycin is a common choice; fluoroquinolones where appropriate); loperamide can be added if non-bloody and afebrile.

cards.prompt: How does ileal resection size change the diarrhea type?
cards.answer: A smaller resection spills bile acids into the colon → secretory bile-acid diarrhea (treat with cholestyramine). A large resection (>~100 cm) depletes the bile-acid pool → steatorrhea.

cards.prompt: What dietary intervention helps many IBS patients?
cards.answer: A trial of a low-FODMAP diet (reducing fermentable carbohydrates), ideally guided by a dietitian, alongside reassurance, exercise, and stress management.

cards.prompt: Why may albumin be low in active IBD?
cards.answer: Severe inflammation (negative acute-phase reactant), poor intake, and protein loss through inflamed bowel all lower albumin — a marker of disease severity.

cards.prompt: How is the gastrocolic reflex used in constipation management?
cards.answer: Encourage a toileting routine shortly after meals, when the gastrocolic reflex naturally increases colonic motility — combined with fiber, fluids, and exercise.

cards.prompt: When should C. difficile be suspected in diarrhea?
cards.answer: With recent antibiotics or hospitalization (and in healthcare settings); avoid anti-motility agents in severe disease, test stool for toxin/PCR, and treat with oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin.

cards.prompt: Why use a low-dose tricyclic in pain-predominant IBS?
cards.answer: Tricyclics act as neuromodulators for visceral hypersensitivity/gut-brain dysfunction, reducing pain at doses lower than antidepressant doses (and slowing transit, useful in IBS-D).

guides.title: Week 30 · 1 Approach to Diarrhea & Mechanisms
guides.html:
Framework

Approach to Diarrhea & Mechanisms

Week 30 · GI II — Diarrhea & Lower GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The two questions that organize every diarrhea (duration, blood), the mimics to exclude, and the four mechanistic types.

Two questions classify it

Classify by duration (acute <4 weeks, chronic >4 weeks) and visible blood. That gives four buckets: acute non-bloody, acute bloody, chronic non-bloody, chronic bloody.

Exclude the mimics first
Before labelling true diarrhea, rule out: overflow diarrhea from constipation/fecal impaction (classic in frail older adults), fecal incontinence, and hyperdefecation (e.g. thyrotoxicosis). These change management entirely.

The four mechanisms

TypeMechanismClueExamples
OsmoticUnabsorbed solute draws water inImproves with fasting; diet-relatedCarbohydrate malabsorption, laxatives
SecretoryIncreased secretion / decreased absorptionPersists despite fasting; large-volume wateryEnterotoxins, bile-acid diarrhea, endocrine
InflammatoryMucosal injuryBlood, fever, pain; fecal WBC/calprotectin, ↑CRPIBD, invasive infection, ischemic colitis
Fatty (steatorrhea)Fat malabsorption/maldigestionGreasy, floating, foul, hard to flushCeliac, chronic pancreatitis, ileal resection
Acute vs chronic and bloody vs non-bloody first. Osmotic stops with fasting; secretory doesn't. Blood/fever/pain = inflammatory → don't reach for loperamide.

guides.title: Week 30 · 2 Acute Diarrhea: Non-Bloody & Bloody
guides.html:
Acute

Acute Diarrhea: Non-Bloody & Bloody

Week 30 · GI II — Diarrhea & Lower GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Supportive care for the common watery case, the invasive-colitis red flags, and the cardinal rule about anti-motility drugs.

Acute non-bloody (usually self-limited)

Most are infectious (viral/toxin) and self-limited. In hospitalized patients also consider C. difficile, drug-induced diarrhea, tube feeds, and overflow from impaction. Investigate (stool testing) only with severe/persistent disease, immunocompromise, outbreak/public-health concern, or recent antibiotics.

  • Rehydrate: oral rehydration solution first-line; IV fluids if severe or can't tolerate oral.
  • Advance diet as tolerated; temporarily limit dairy, very sugary/high-fat foods, roughage, alcohol, coffee if they worsen symptoms.
  • Anti-motility (loperamide, diphenoxylate/atropine) is reasonable for non-bloody, non-invasive diarrhea. Bismuth subsalicylate has anti-secretory/anti-motility/antimicrobial effects (darkens stool).

Acute bloody = inflammatory/invasive until proven otherwise

Causes: Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, Yersinia, invasive or Shiga-toxin E. coli (STEC/O157), Entamoeba histolytica (exposure history), ischemic colitis, and first-presentation IBD.

Two rules for bloody diarrhea
1) Avoid anti-motility agents (loperamide/diphenoxylate) in bloody diarrhea or suspected invasive colitis — they can worsen toxic megacolon and, with STEC, increase hemolytic-uremic syndrome risk. 2) Avoid empiric antibiotics in suspected STEC (may increase HUS). Get a stool culture/PCR; targeted therapy when indicated. Azithromycin is common for moderate-severe traveler's diarrhea.
Watery + no red flags → ORS ± loperamide. Bloody/febrile → hydrate, culture, no anti-motility; suspect STEC (don't give antibiotics or anti-motility, watch for HUS).

guides.title: Week 30 · 3 Chronic Diarrhea & Malabsorption
guides.html:
Chronic

Chronic Diarrhea & Malabsorption

Week 30 · GI II — Diarrhea & Lower GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The broad differential, the baseline workup including fecal calprotectin, and the absorption map that predicts each deficiency.

Differential

Broad: carbohydrate malabsorption, IBS, celiac, Crohn, microscopic colitis, bile-acid malabsorption, chronic infection (Giardia), medications, and overflow.

Baseline workup

  • CBC, electrolytes, renal function, albumin, ferritin, CRP, ± B12.
  • Celiac serology: tissue transglutaminase IgA with total IgA (to catch IgA deficiency).
  • TSH/fT4 if hyperdefecation/thyroid disease suspected.
  • Fecal calprotectin separates inflammatory (IBD) from functional (IBS) disease — a key gatekeeper test.

Where things are absorbed (predicts the deficiency)

Region / factorAbsorbsIf lost
DuodenumIron, calciumCeliac → iron-deficiency anemia, bone disease
JejunumMacronutrients, Na, waterORS works via Na-glucose cotransport here
Terminal ileumB12, bile acidsCrohn/resection → B12 deficiency, bile-acid diarrhea or steatorrhea
Exocrine pancreasEnzymes + bicarbonateChronic pancreatitis → maldigestion/steatorrhea
Bile-acid handling
A large terminal-ileal resection (>~100 cm) depletes the bile-acid pool → impaired micelle formation → steatorrhea. A smaller resection spills bile acids into the colon → secretory bile-acid diarrhea (treat with a sequestrant like cholestyramine).
Fecal calprotectin triages inflammatory vs functional. Duodenum = iron/calcium (celiac), terminal ileum = B12/bile acids (Crohn). Greasy floating stool = fat malabsorption.

guides.title: Week 30 · 4 Celiac Disease
guides.html:
Gluten

Celiac Disease

Week 30 · GI II — Diarrhea & Lower GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Why the duodenum drives the presentation, the test-while-eating-gluten rule, and lifelong management.

What it is

Immune-mediated small-intestinal injury triggered by gluten, concentrated in the duodenum — which is where iron and calcium are preferentially absorbed. So the high-yield presentations are iron-deficiency anemia, osteopenia/osteoporosis, fatigue, bloating, and chronic non-bloody diarrhea (which may be subtle or absent).

Associations

  • Other autoimmune disease (autoimmune thyroid), type 1 diabetes, family history, dermatitis herpetiformis.

Diagnosis

Test while eating gluten
First-line serology is tissue transglutaminase IgA + total IgA; confirm with duodenal biopsy (villous atrophy) when indicated. The patient must still be eating gluten — starting a gluten-free diet before testing normalizes serology and histology and obscures the diagnosis.

Beyond the gut

  • Dermatitis herpetiformis — intensely itchy grouped vesicles on extensor surfaces; the skin manifestation of celiac (responds to a gluten-free diet ± dapsone).
  • Other clues: transaminitis, recurrent aphthous ulcers, infertility/recurrent miscarriage, peripheral neuropathy, and hyposplenism.

Management

  • Lifelong gluten-free diet (the only treatment), ideally with dietitian support.
  • Assess and replace deficiencies: iron, calcium, vitamin D, folate, B12; consider a baseline bone-density scan.
  • Pneumococcal vaccination if hyposplenic.
  • Monitor symptoms and a falling tTG-IgA as a marker of dietary adherence.
Persistent symptoms despite a gluten-free diet
The commonest cause is ongoing gluten exposure (hidden sources, cross-contamination) — recheck adherence first. Consider co-existing conditions (lactose intolerance, microscopic colitis, pancreatic insufficiency). True refractory celiac disease is rare and raises concern for enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL) — refer.
Iron-deficiency anemia + an autoimmune background (thyroid, T1DM) + bloating = think celiac. Order tTG-IgA with total IgA, don't go gluten-free before testing, and if symptoms persist on the diet, suspect hidden gluten before rare refractory disease/EATL.

guides.title: Week 30 · 5 Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS)
guides.html:
Gut-brain

Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS)

Week 30 · GI II — Diarrhea & Lower GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The Rome-IV definition, the alarm features that exclude it, and subtype-directed therapy.

Definition

A disorder of gut-brain interaction: recurrent abdominal pain (Rome IV: ≥1 day/week over the last 3 months, onset ≥6 months) associated with ≥2 of: relation to defecation, change in stool frequency, change in stool form. Subtypes: IBS-D, IBS-C, IBS-M.

Alarm features argue against IBS
Rectal bleeding, anemia, unintentional weight loss, nocturnal symptoms, elevated inflammatory markers, family history of IBD/colorectal cancer, or persistent symptoms despite therapy → investigate for organic disease instead.

Reasonable limited testing (no alarms)

CBC, CRP or fecal calprotectin, and celiac serology (especially non-constipation subtypes). Extensive testing isn't needed when the picture fits and alarms are absent.

Management

SubtypeOptions
AllEducation/reassurance, low-FODMAP trial, exercise, sleep/stress management
IBS-DLoperamide for frequency; bile-acid binder if bile-acid diarrhea; antispasmodics; rifaximin or neuromodulators in selected cases
IBS-CSoluble fiber, PEG, secretagogues (linaclotide, plecanatide, lubiprostone)
Pain-predominantAntispasmodics, peppermint oil, low-dose tricyclic or other neuromodulators
IBS = chronic pain tied to defecation/stool changes with NO alarm features and normal calprotectin. Treat by subtype; loperamide helps the diarrhea but not the pain (use a neuromodulator for that).

guides.title: Week 30 · 6 Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn vs UC
guides.html:
IBD

Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Crohn vs UC

Week 30 · GI II — Diarrhea & Lower GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The anatomy and depth that separate Crohn from ulcerative colitis, the red flags, and the extraintestinal clues.

Crohn vs ulcerative colitis

FeatureCrohn diseaseUlcerative colitis
LocationMouth→anus; often terminal ileum / right colonColon only; starts in rectum
PatternSkip lesionsContinuous
DepthTransmuralMucosal/submucosal
StoolChronic non-bloody commonChronic bloody common; urgency/tenesmus
Perianal diseaseFistulae, abscesses, tags, stricturesUncommon
ComplicationsStrictures, fistulas, abscesses, malabsorptionToxic megacolon, colorectal cancer risk
SurgeryNot curative (recurs)Colectomy can be curative for colitis

Red flags & markers

Weight loss, nocturnal diarrhea, anemia, blood, fever, perianal disease, ↑CRP, low albumin, family history. Stool WBC/RBC, fecal calprotectin, and CRP suggest inflammation but aren't diagnostic alone. Diagnosis is by colonoscopy with biopsies (± cross-sectional imaging for Crohn).

Extraintestinal manifestations

  • Aphthous ulcers, episcleritis/uveitis, erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum, peripheral/axial arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, and primary sclerosing cholangitis (especially UC).
Crohn = skip lesions, transmural, terminal ileum/right colon, perianal disease, fistulas. UC = continuous, mucosal, rectum-upward, bloody, toxic-megacolon/cancer risk, curable by colectomy.

guides.title: Week 30 · 7 IBD Pharmacology
guides.html:
IBD drugs

IBD Pharmacology

Week 30 · GI II — Diarrhea & Lower GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The induction-vs-maintenance logic, the biologic classes, and the safety screening each one needs.

Induction vs maintenance

Steroids induce, they don't maintain
Corticosteroids (prednisone; IV in severe disease) rapidly induce remission but are not for maintenance — long-term toxicity (osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, hyperglycemia, infection, Cushingoid changes, avascular necrosis). Plan a steroid-sparing maintenance agent from the start.

The agents

ClassExamplesRole / cautions
5-ASAMesalamine (oral/topical)Mainly mild-moderate UC; little benefit in Crohn
ImmunomodulatorsAzathioprine/6-MP; methotrexate (Crohn)Steroid-sparing maintenance; myelosuppression, hepatotoxicity; check TPMT; MTX teratogenic
Anti-TNF biologicsInfliximab, adalimumab, certolizumabInduction + maintenance; fistulizing disease; screen TB and hepatitis B first; infection risk
Anti-integrinVedolizumabGut-selective → less systemic immunosuppression
Anti-IL (12/23, 23)Ustekinumab; risankizumabModerate-severe disease; infection risk

Practical points

  • Before any biologic/immunomodulator: screen for latent TB and hepatitis B, update vaccinations (live vaccines while immunosuppressed are contraindicated).
  • UC refractory to medical therapy → colectomy (curative for colitis); Crohn surgery is for complications and is not curative.
Steroids = induction only. 5-ASA is a UC drug. Anti-TNF agents need TB/hepatitis-B screening. Vedolizumab is gut-selective. Choose maintenance early to get patients off steroids.

guides.title: Week 30 · 8 Constipation & Fecal Incontinence
guides.html:
Lower tract

Constipation & Fecal Incontinence

Week 30 · GI II — Diarrhea & Lower GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The secondary causes to exclude, the laxative classes by mechanism, and the overflow-diarrhea trap in older adults.

Constipation

Reduced frequency, hard stools, straining, incomplete evacuation, or manual maneuvers. Separate primary from secondary causes.

Secondary causes to exclude
Drugs (opioids, iron, anticholinergics, calcium-channel blockers), hypothyroidism, hypercalcemia, diabetes, Parkinson disease, spinal-cord disease, structural obstruction, and defecatory disorders. Alarm features (bleeding, weight loss, anemia, new onset in older age) → investigate for colorectal cancer.

Laxatives by class

ClassExamplesNote
Bulk-formingPsylliumIncrease slowly (bloating)
OsmoticPEG 3350, lactulose, magnesiumMagnesium → diarrhea; caution in renal disease
StimulantSenna, bisacodylStimulate enteric motility
SecretagoguesLinaclotide, lubiprostoneChronic constipation / IBS-C
ProkineticPrucalopride (5-HT4 agonist)Refractory slow transit
Opioid-induced (OIC)Naloxegol, methylnaltrexonePeripheral opioid antagonists

Non-drug: gradual fiber + fluids, exercise, a toileting routine using the gastrocolic reflex after meals.

Fecal incontinence

  • Involuntary stool passage (age >4); common in older adults / long-term care.
  • Mechanisms: sphincter weakness (obstetric/pelvic injury, diabetes, scleroderma), impaired rectal storage/sensation (dementia, radiation), or a bowel disturbance.
The overflow trap
An older adult with new "diarrhea" + incontinence and a constipation history = fecal impaction with overflow until proven otherwise (iron worsens it). Do a rectal exam — disimpact, don't give loperamide.
Always screen constipation for secondary causes (opioids, hypothyroid, hypercalcemia). New diarrhea/incontinence in a constipated elder = overflow from impaction.

guides.title: Week 30 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

GI II — Diarrhea & Lower GI

Week 30 · GI II — Diarrhea & Lower GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Bloody diarrhea / suspected invasive colitis → NO anti-motility agents; hydrate + stool culture
Suspected Shiga-toxin E. coli (O157) → avoid antibiotics AND anti-motility; monitor for hemolytic-uremic syndrome
Severe UC with colonic dilation + toxicity → toxic megacolon — surgical emergency; stop opioids/anti-motility
Older adult, constipation history, new 'diarrhea'/incontinence → fecal impaction with overflow (rectal exam, disimpact)
Don't go gluten-free before celiac testing → serology/biopsy normalize and obscure the diagnosis
Prednisone in IBD → induction only, never maintenance

Approach Framework

Classify acute/chronic + bloody/non-bloody
Mimics overflow, incontinence, hyperdefecation
Osmotic improves with fasting
Secretory persists with fasting
Inflammatory blood/fever/pain, ↑calprotectin/CRP
Fatty greasy, floating, foul

Acute diarrhea Acute

Non-bloody ORS ± loperamide; usually self-limited
Bloody invasive — no anti-motility; culture
STEC/O157 no abx, no anti-motility; HUS risk
Traveler's azithromycin if moderate-severe
Hospitalized think C. difficile

Chronic / malabsorption Chronic

Calprotectin inflammatory vs functional
Duodenum iron/calcium (celiac)
Terminal ileum B12 / bile acids (Crohn)
Small resection bile-acid diarrhea → cholestyramine
Large resection steatorrhea

Celiac Celiac

Presentation iron-deficiency anemia, bloating, bone disease
Associations autoimmune thyroid, T1DM
Test tTG-IgA + total IgA, ON gluten
Confirm duodenal biopsy (villous atrophy)
Treat lifelong gluten-free; replace iron/Ca/D/folate/B12

IBS IBS

Rome IV pain + ≥2 stool-related features, no alarms
Alarms bleed, anemia, weight loss, nocturnal
Diet low-FODMAP trial
IBS-D loperamide, bile-acid binder, rifaximin
IBS-C fiber, PEG, linaclotide
Pain neuromodulator (low-dose TCA)

IBD IBD

Crohn skip, transmural, ileum/right colon, perianal
UC continuous, mucosal, rectum-up, bloody
UC complication toxic megacolon, CRC risk
EIM uveitis, erythema nodosum, PSC, arthritis
Steroids induction only
Anti-TNF screen TB + hepatitis B

Constipation / incontinence Lower

Secondary opioids, iron, hypothyroid, hypercalcemia
Bulk/osmotic/stimulant psyllium / PEG / senna
Secretagogue linaclotide
Prokinetic prucalopride (5-HT4)
OIC naloxegol / methylnaltrexone
Overflow impaction in elders → disimpact
